title: Homer Goes To Prep School

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Opening moment of episode
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation of frustration
jokes.explanation: Iconic character catchphrase that's pure Homer. High character integration and economy, but no originality since it's established.

jokes.text: Homer, I told you, don't call me 'Mom.' Sorry, Mrs. Simpson.
jokes.setup: Homer addresses his wife as 'Mom' like his children do
jokes.punchline: Homer formally apologizes and calls her Mrs. Simpson
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Homer's childlike mentality and literal interpretation of instructions.

jokes.text: This isn't a learn zone. Darn it, it's a fun place!
jokes.setup: Kids are disappointed about going to Learn Zone
jokes.punchline: Homer's fake dismay that it's actually fun instead of educational
jokes.explanation: Mild irony about Homer preferring fun over education, fits his character but not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: Is this a kid you'd pay ransom for?
jokes.setup: Employee explaining anti-kidnapping bracelets for safety
jokes.punchline: Casually asking if the child is worth kidnapping
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's surprisingly blunt. Good economy and originality for a throwaway line.

jokes.text: Dad, what would you do if you got my ear in the mail? I don't know. Feed it to the dog?
jokes.setup: Lisa asking a hypothetical about ransom situations
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual, practical response about feeding it to the dog
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristically inappropriate and practical response to a dark scenario.

jokes.text: You'd have to wrap cheese around it. Don't you tell me how to feed you to the dog!
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting how to make her ear more appetizing to the dog
jokes.punchline: Homer's indignant response about his dog-feeding expertise
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that gets funnier as it gets more absurd. Homer's wounded pride about being corrected on feeding body parts to pets is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Welcome to the Mormon Church, America's most respectable cult.
jokes.setup: Children going through play tubes
jokes.punchline: Sign or announcement describing Mormonism as a respectable cult
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical jab at religion with good economy, but feels like a random pop culture reference.

jokes.text: Keep an eye on the kids, Homer. Can I keep an eye on that kid?
jokes.setup: Marge asks Homer to watch their kids
jokes.punchline: Homer asks to watch a different, presumably attractive kid instead
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer misdirection but feels somewhat predictable and potentially inappropriate.

jokes.text: He's fabulous. Bart and Lisa. Oh! It's always my kids.
jokes.setup: Homer commenting on another child, then being told to find his own
jokes.punchline: Homer's exasperated complaint that it's always his responsibility to watch his own children
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - being annoyed at having to parent his own children. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Dad, come up, come up! Pretty, pretty please! Absolutely not! My adult frame is simply too large.
jokes.setup: Bart begging Homer to come up in the play structure
jokes.punchline: Homer's formal, overly dignified refusal citing his 'adult frame'
jokes.explanation: Homer's pompous way of saying he's too fat to fit. Sets up the physical comedy to come.

jokes.text: Come on, Dad! No! Never! Come on, Dad! Fine. Here I come. Crawling. So... painful. Duck walk. Infinitely worse. Ooh!
jokes.setup: Homer initially refusing to enter the play structure
jokes.punchline: Homer's play-by-play narration of his increasingly painful movement through child-sized spaces
jokes.explanation: Great physical comedy sequence where Homer narrates his own suffering. The escalation from crawling to duck walk works well.

jokes.text: Only wanted... to be fun. Shh, shh, shh. Soon you'll know peace.
jokes.setup: Homer trapped and suffering in the play structure
jokes.punchline: Homer speaking to himself like he's dying, then comforting himself about imminent death
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny moment where Homer treats being stuck in a play structure like a death scene. Good absurdist humor.

jokes.text: I made it, kids! I am king of Learnington Castle!
jokes.setup: Homer finally making it through the play structure after great suffering
jokes.punchline: Homer's triumphant declaration of victory in a children's playground
jokes.explanation: Homer's childlike pride in conquering a children's obstacle. Good character moment but fairly predictable payoff.

jokes.text: We tricked you. Eat Nerf, Homeboy!
jokes.setup: Homer expecting praise from his children for reaching them
jokes.punchline: Kids reveal it was a setup and attack him with Nerf weapons
jokes.explanation: Standard kids-outsmarting-parent scenario with a mildly funny battle cry.

jokes.text: What cool kid thing's in here? No kids. That's what's cool.
jokes.setup: Homer looking for something appealing in a kid-free area
jokes.punchline: Realizing the absence of children is itself the appeal
jokes.explanation: Relatable parent humor about appreciating kid-free spaces. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: I have a phone. It's very tiny.
jokes.setup: Child showing off their phone
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact description of phone as 'very tiny'
jokes.explanation: Weak joke relying on a child's simple observation. Minimal comedy value.

jokes.text: Ain't no shame in being poor, boy. What's pitiful is if you is ignorant. Is these mice pillows here for the taking?
jokes.setup: Willie giving advice about not being ashamed of poverty vs ignorance
jokes.punchline: Willie immediately demonstrating ignorance with poor grammar and thinking cushions are mice
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic reversal where Willie immediately contradicts his own wisdom. Perfect character voice and strong structure.

jokes.text: I hate working here. At least at Krustyburger, you could burn yourself and go home.
jokes.setup: Employee expressing job dissatisfaction
jokes.punchline: Preferring a job where injury was an escape option
jokes.explanation: Dark workplace humor with good economy. The specificity of 'burn yourself and go home' makes it memorable.

jokes.text: Another stupid kid got out. I can't tell the manager. I am the manager. Duh!
jokes.setup: Employee worried about reporting to manager
jokes.punchline: Realization they are the manager, followed by self-directed 'duh'
jokes.explanation: Simple realization joke with decent structure. The 'duh' adds a nice touch of self-awareness.

jokes.text: These lasers are nothing more than colored lights!
jokes.setup: Security lockdown with apparent laser barriers
jokes.punchline: Lisa's logical observation deflating the dramatic tension
jokes.explanation: Very Lisa-like moment of rational observation cutting through manufactured drama.

jokes.text: Until then, why don't we bond by sharing our childbirth experiences. The nurses were so great. My husband held my hand the whole time. See? All you need to get through a crisis is a little friendly conversation. Bet you the dads are doing the same thing.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting civilized conversation to pass time, with saccharine example
jokes.punchline: Assumption that the men are having equally civil discourse
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the contrast with the men's behavior. Structure works well for the payoff.

jokes.text: The sneeze guard is broken! There is no law! Oh, I just came here to pick up my daughter. But now I'm gonna kill you all!
jokes.setup: Men locked in during emergency
jokes.punchline: Escalation from broken sneeze guard to anarchy to casual mention of daughter pickup to murder threat
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from minor broken equipment to total societal breakdown. The casual daughter pickup line makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Get out of there, you! / Never! / Mmm. Butter.
jokes.setup: Someone trying to get Homer out of somewhere
jokes.punchline: Homer refusing and enjoying butter
jokes.explanation: Homer's food obsession overriding everything else

jokes.text: Apes. Deep down, we're all savage apes. Leave your body, Homer.
jokes.setup: Homer observing the chaos around him
jokes.punchline: Philosophical observation followed by telling himself to astral project
jokes.explanation: Homer's pseudo-philosophical moment with typical absurd conclusion. Good economy but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, I've taken eight months off. It's time to get back on the horse.
jokes.setup: Marge asking if Homer should go to work after trauma
jokes.punchline: Homer treating an 8-month absence as if it's reasonable prep time
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer work ethic joke - treating months of absence as responsible recovery time.

jokes.text: Hey, neighbor, your welcome mat's looking a little dusty. Mind if I come over and sweeten your greetin'. Sweeten your greetin'! Sweeten your greetin'. Three Neds are better than one!
jokes.setup: Ned offering to clean Homer's welcome mat
jokes.punchline: Ned's overly cheerful wordplay and multiplication into three versions of himself
jokes.explanation: Very Ned-like cheerful wordplay, though the multiplication feels like filler. Strong character voice.

jokes.text: I hate to be a nag, but this is my real head.
jokes.setup: Ned apparently having multiple heads in Homer's hallucination
jokes.punchline: Ned politely pointing out which head is actually his
jokes.explanation: Excellent surreal visual gag with perfect Ned politeness. Great economy and originality.

jokes.text: anarchy lurks around every corner, like a racially-diverse street gang on a network cop show.
jokes.setup: Homer describing the threat of anarchy
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to the predictable diversity casting of TV crime shows
jokes.explanation: Sharp media satire about formulaic TV casting. Good originality though slightly forced into Homer's voice.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, read the sign, pal, no acronyms. You see? And that goes for the rest of youse, too! Okay, okay. Hey, in this bar, we say 'Old Kinderhook!'
jokes.setup: Moe enforcing a no-acronyms rule
jokes.punchline: Moe's alternative to 'OK' is an obscure historical reference that's actually longer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd rule that defeats its own purpose. Perfect Moe authoritarian tendencies with historical knowledge.

jokes.text: America can't collapse! We're as powerful as ancient Rome!
jokes.setup: Barney defending America's permanence
jokes.punchline: Comparing America to Rome, which famously collapsed
jokes.explanation: Classic historical irony that works well. Good structure and economy though not particularly original.

jokes.text: Close the supermarket, and we starve. Cut off the tap, we drink our cat's blood.
jokes.setup: Prepper describing modern dependency
jokes.punchline: Escalation from starvation to drinking cat blood
jokes.explanation: Good escalation to an absurd extreme. The cat blood detail is memorably specific.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! This unsourced, undated video has convinced me beyond any doubt!
jokes.setup: Homer watching prepper propaganda
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate total conviction despite obvious lack of credibility
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of gullibility and misinformation. Perfect Homer logic and very relevant social commentary.

jokes.text: Hey, man, no need to almost swear.
jokes.setup: Someone saying 'when the stuff hits the fan'
jokes.punchline: Gentle correction about almost using profanity
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about euphemisms. Decent but not memorable.

jokes.text: Well, Homer's gone. Let's all go into our suspended state till he gets back.
jokes.setup: Homer leaving the family
jokes.punchline: Marge acknowledging the family's cartoon existence with deadpan acceptance
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about animated characters existing only when relevant to plot. Great economy and originality.

jokes.text: Preppies? Oh, I hate you guys! Always partying on deck with your Docksiders while us townies are scrubbing the bilge!
jokes.setup: Homer hearing 'preppers' as 'preppies'
jokes.punchline: Homer's class resentment against prep school stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with specific nautical imagery that fits Homer's working-class resentment.

jokes.text: Not preppies. Preppers. But I like your hatred. It's based on nothing. That's super.
jokes.setup: Correcting Homer's misunderstanding
jokes.punchline: Appreciating Homer's baseless hatred as a positive quality
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about baseless prejudice being valued. Good economy and deadpan delivery.

jokes.text: I'd slash my wrists. But how, without my electric knife?!
jokes.setup: Asked what he'd do during an electromagnetic pulse
jokes.punchline: Homer's suicidal plan thwarted by dependence on electric appliances
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that perfectly captures Homer's logic and modern dependence on technology.

jokes.text: There is an alternative to suicide. Mm. I can't conceive of any.
jokes.setup: Prepper offering hope beyond suicide
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact inability to imagine alternatives
jokes.explanation: Good Homer fatalism delivered with characteristic deadpan. Fits his pessimistic worldview.

jokes.text: Don't worry. I'm very good at keeping secrets.
jokes.setup: Told to keep prepping secret
jokes.punchline: Homer claiming to be good at secrets (setting up inevitable revelation)
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for Homer's inability to keep secrets. Predictable but functional.

jokes.text: 'Impending doom.' What you reading, Dad? Honey, everything's fine. There's nothing to worry about. When grown-ups say that, it means there is something to worry about.
jokes.setup: Lisa noticing Homer's reading material and his reassurance
jokes.punchline: Lisa's wise observation about adult psychology
jokes.explanation: Good child wisdom about adult behavior. Very Lisa-like insight with solid structure.

jokes.text: Just go play your saxophone. While you can. What does that mean? Secrets? I have no secrets! Just enjoy this golden time you will soon cling desperately to the memory of. Smell some bread!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to act normal while failing to keep secrets
jokes.punchline: Escalating ominous warnings ending with desperate distraction attempt
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Homer's inability to keep secrets. 'Smell some bread!' is a perfect absurd distraction.

jokes.text: Typical sheeple. Uh... are you eating my grain?
jokes.setup: Prepper criticizing regular people as 'sheeple'
jokes.punchline: Immediate concern about Homer eating his supplies
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection from philosophy to immediate practical concern. Very Homer behavior.

jokes.text: That was a five-pound bag! Yeah, but I'll go home, weigh myself, and I'll have gained six pounds. What's that about?
jokes.setup: Prepper upset about Homer eating five pounds of grain
jokes.punchline: Homer's observation about mysteriously gaining more weight than food consumed
jokes.explanation: Relatable observation about weight gain mysteries. Good Homer logic about food math not working.

jokes.text: Guys who call themselves Preacher or Deacon are very bad. Water is money, unless gasoline is money. And even though lots of things are razor-sharp, no one ever shaves.
jokes.setup: Bart summarizing lessons from post-apocalyptic movies
jokes.punchline: Specific observations about movie tropes, especially the shaving paradox
jokes.explanation: Excellent genre parody with specific, observant details. The shaving observation is particularly clever.

jokes.text: Now the Ice Bishop of Beverly Hills will teach you the true meaning of cold! I have come from a place where the land is warm, and the only ice is made by machines called... fridge-ra-torrs. Fridge-ra-torrs! Fridge-ra-torrs! Fridge-ra-torrs!
jokes.setup: Post-apocalyptic movie villain giving dramatic speech
jokes.punchline: Treating 'refrigerators' as an exotic foreign word worthy of chanting
jokes.explanation: Decent parody of post-apocalyptic drama, but the joke relies mainly on pronunciation rather than deeper insight.

jokes.text: Aren't they just saying 'refrigerator'? Do not question the wisdom of Tom Skerritt.
jokes.setup: Lisa making logical observation about the movie
jokes.punchline: Homer's reverent defense of B-movie actor authority
jokes.explanation: Great meta-humor about movie logic and celebrity authority. Perfect Homer reverence for questionable sources.

jokes.text: Wow! Your end of the world is better than my during the world.
jokes.setup: Homer seeing the preppers' luxurious bunker
jokes.punchline: Comparing 'end of the world' to 'during the world' living conditions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that perfectly captures Homer's economic situation. Excellent economy and very quotable.

jokes.text: you're not just being nice to me because in a pinch you could make candles from my fat? Well, that is a big part of it. It's okay. I know what I am.
jokes.setup: Homer asking if his value is purely utilitarian
jokes.punchline: Honest admission followed by Homer's self-aware acceptance
jokes.explanation: Dark but touching moment of self-awareness. The candle fat detail is specific and memorable.

jokes.text: You can't have secrets from your wife! / It's very late in the marriage to tell me that.
jokes.setup: Marge insisting on no secrets
jokes.punchline: Homer pointing out the timing of this revelation
jokes.explanation: Great marriage humor with perfect timing observation

jokes.text: I thought the basement looked cleaner.
jokes.setup: Discovering Homer has hidden supplies behind fake wall
jokes.punchline: Marge's understated observation about improved cleanliness
jokes.explanation: Typical Marge practical observation. Good character voice but mild impact.

jokes.text: There's no handle on this side!
jokes.setup: Homer showing off his prepper preparations
jokes.punchline: Revealing a major design flaw in his secret bunker
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reveal of Homer's poor planning. Typical oversight that undermines his preparation.

jokes.text: There's no handle on this side!
jokes.setup: Marge going to bed after dismissing Homer's preparations
jokes.punchline: Immediately needing the bunker door to work properly
jokes.explanation: Ironic immediate consequence of Homer's poor planning

jokes.text: The one thing I couldn't prepare for.
jokes.setup: Homer trapped by his own bunker design
jokes.punchline: Treating basic door functionality as an unpredictable variable
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic about his own incompetence. Makes his failure seem like an act of God.

jokes.text: I will eat anything orange except an orange.
jokes.setup: Homer categorizing his snack foods
jokes.punchline: Refusing to eat the actual fruit while accepting artificial orange foods
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer food logic with excellent economy. Captures American processed food preferences brilliantly.

jokes.text: An E.M.P.?! You know what that means! Hand scissors.
jokes.setup: Electromagnetic pulse disabling all electronics
jokes.punchline: Homer's solution is manual scissors instead of electric
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier electric knife joke. Shows Homer's preparation focusing on his earlier concern.

jokes.text: Mr. Mayor, what's your administration doing to ensure there's no panic? I don't know. What is your administration doing? There you have it. A town without rules, without leadership, without television. This is Kent Brockman, talking to himself.
jokes.setup: News reporter asking mayor about crisis response
jokes.punchline: Mayor deflecting question back, reporter acknowledging he's talking to himself
jokes.explanation: Good political satire with meta-awareness. The escalation to self-conversation works well.

jokes.text: Marge, this is it, TEOTWAWKI! 'The end of the world as we know it'? Uh-huh.
jokes.setup: Homer using prepper acronym
jokes.punchline: Marge immediately decoding the unnecessarily complex abbreviation
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of prepper culture acronyms. Marge's quick translation deflates Homer's dramatic moment.

jokes.text: Boy, get in the car, and say good-bye to your best friend forever. Bye forever, Milhouse. See you, Bart.
jokes.setup: Homer's dramatic farewell instructions
jokes.punchline: Casual 'see you' response to 'goodbye forever'
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between adult drama and child practicality. Milhouse's casual response deflates the moment perfectly.

jokes.text: Is that sax alto or baritone? Baritone. This is gonna be a long apocalypse.
jokes.setup: Homer presenting Lisa as bringing music to the new world
jokes.punchline: Prepper's dismay at the specific type of saxophone
jokes.explanation: Funny deflation of romantic post-apocalyptic ideals. The specific saxophone concern is absurdly petty.

jokes.text: Write down which celebrities were rumored to be gay for future generations. Yes, sir. Society will not have to start from square one.
jokes.setup: Homer assigning tasks to preserve civilization
jokes.punchline: Treating celebrity gossip as essential cultural knowledge
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of what people consider important cultural knowledge. Good absurdist priorities.

jokes.text: Well, just because I imagined it, doesn't make it any less true.
jokes.setup: Marge pointing out no one was laughing at Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's logic about imaginary events being equally valid
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic about reality and imagination. Great character insight with good economy.

jokes.text: Well, just because I imagined it, doesn't make it any less true.
jokes.setup: Homer being caught in his lie
jokes.punchline: Defending imaginary mockery as real
jokes.explanation: Homer's twisted logic about reality and imagination

jokes.text: If Jesus had a gun, he'd be alive today.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning Homer's Christian values
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted logic about Jesus and firearms
jokes.explanation: Controversial religious humor with great economy. Typical American gun culture satire through Homer.

jokes.text: When I fell in love with you, it was because of your big heart and your good looks. But some day, those good looks are gonna fade. What?!
jokes.setup: Marge giving romantic speech about Homer's qualities
jokes.punchline: Homer's shock at the suggestion his looks will fade
jokes.explanation: Good relationship humor about Homer's self-image. His shock at aging is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: But some day, those good looks are gonna fade. / What?!
jokes.setup: Marge saying she loves Homer for his heart and looks
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning his looks will fade, Homer's shock
jokes.explanation: Homer's vanity and denial about aging

jokes.text: This rifle scope I modified makes any human face look dangerous and threatening. Boom.
jokes.setup: Prepper explaining how to avoid empathy
jokes.punchline: Technical solution to make everyone appear threatening
jokes.explanation: Dark satire about dehumanization technology. Creative premise though execution is straightforward.

jokes.text: Why do we have to shoot them at all? Are you that good with a knife? No! I want to help the people who didn't prepare.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning the need for violence
jokes.punchline: Prepper assuming she wants alternative weapon rather than peace
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing prepper mindset vs. Marge's compassion. Dark but effective contrast.

jokes.text: You were thinking I'd gone mad with power, weren't you?! / I wasn't, but I am now.
jokes.setup: Prepper asking if Homer thinks he's power mad
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest response creating the feared outcome
jokes.explanation: Great escalation where honesty creates the problem

jokes.text: I've got to be honest with you. It was just a bag of rice I drew a face on. White rice or brown? I never noticed.
jokes.setup: Homer mentioning Bart's bug-out camp girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Revealing it was rice with a face, followed by Homer's casual question about rice type
jokes.explanation: Good absurd revelation followed by Homer's practical food concern. The rice type question is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: If we miss it, we're dead. / Dad, there are lights following us. / How can that be? I took every last can of gas.
jokes.setup: Homer confident about his gas theft
jokes.punchline: Evidence that others still have transportation
jokes.explanation: Homer's overconfidence in his planning

jokes.text: Now, everyone relax and pretend we're about to be hit by a soft brick wall.
jokes.setup: Car chase/crash situation
jokes.punchline: Homer's attempt at reassuring crash preparation advice
jokes.explanation: Absurd crash preparation advice. The 'soft brick wall' contradiction is typical Homer logic.

jokes.text: The starving people of Springfield are desperately in need of our delivery of canned corn, corn flakes and flash frozen corn niblets!
jokes.setup: Homer describing their charitable mission
jokes.punchline: Realizing their supplies are all corn-based
jokes.explanation: Homer's lack of dietary variety planning

jokes.text: Now to save these sorry souls from a life of anarchy, starvation and having sex for procreation.
jokes.setup: Homer describing the terrible fate of unprepared people
jokes.punchline: Including procreative sex as one of the horrors
jokes.explanation: Good escalation where procreation is treated as equally horrible as starvation. Reflects modern attitude toward children.

jokes.text: Dad, you said people would be drinking each other's blood. You got to watch a VHS copy of Red Dawn 13 times! Stop complaining!
jokes.setup: Bart complaining about failed apocalypse predictions
jokes.punchline: Homer's defense focusing on Bart getting to watch movies
jokes.explanation: Good Homer parental logic about silver linings. Treats movie watching as adequate compensation for false apocalypse.

jokes.text: The zoo animals weren't eaten? / No-- well, a couple.
jokes.setup: Homer checking on zoo animal survival
jokes.punchline: Casual admission some were eaten anyway
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about partial zoo survival

jokes.text: This non-disaster is a catastrophe!
jokes.setup: Homer learning the world didn't end
jokes.punchline: Treating good news as bad news
jokes.explanation: Homer's disappointment at civilization continuing

jokes.text: This non-disaster is a catastrophe!
jokes.setup: Homer discovering the world didn't end
jokes.punchline: Treating survival as the real disaster
jokes.explanation: Excellent oxymoron with perfect economy. Captures Homer's backward priorities perfectly.

jokes.text: Me and the others were planning to overthrow you and seal you in a cave. Yeah, but what you didn't know was I was gonna poison all your drinking water. Which is why I'm only drinking my own urine.
jokes.setup: Revelation of planned betrayal
jokes.punchline: Counter-betrayal plan leading to self-imposed urine drinking
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of paranoid planning. The urine drinking is a logical but gross conclusion.

jokes.text: Look, you can have potato chips now, or if you wait ten minutes, you can have all the brains you can eat. I want both.
jokes.setup: Zombie Homer offering delayed gratification lesson
jokes.punchline: Zombie Bart wanting both immediate and delayed rewards
jokes.explanation: Decent parody of delayed gratification experiments with zombie twist. Bart's response is predictably greedy.

tmdb_overview: Homer joins the "Springfield Preppers," an off-the-grid survivalist group whose leader has set up a top-secret retreat outside of town to prepare for the end of the world.
